name: w157_skillimp_hybrid
guidance: The response scale is not a positivity scale. Many U.S. adults reserve "Extremely important" for skills they view as essential for nearly all workers, and often choose "Somewhat important" for skills that are useful but not central. Do not make every support type enthusiastic. Keep the support point realistic and use middle categories when the dimension profile calls for moderation.

education: some college or an associate degree
occupation: manual, trade, repair, or physical work
service, care, retail, or hospitality work
office, professional, education, or management work
technology_orientation: skeptical of new workplace technology and worried it displaces useful human skills
pragmatic about workplace technology, valuing it when it clearly helps
enthusiastic about new workplace technology and quick to see productivity gains
skill_worldview: believes basic reliability and communication matter more than specialized technical skills
believes both practical interpersonal skills and technical skills matter
believes advanced analytical and technical skills are increasingly decisive
response_style: rarely calls a skill extremely important unless it is essential for almost every worker
uses the full response scale and often chooses somewhat important for useful but nonessential skills
is broadly positive and often rates many skills very or extremely important
is selective, distinguishing sharply between core skills and niche skills
